request.question: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on which date, Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid?

title: 2000 Belgian Grand Prix

2009 Belgian Grand Prix
Sebastian Vettel
sentences: The 2000 Belgian Grand Prix (formally, the LVIII Foster's Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 27 August 2000 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps, Stavelot, Belgium.
It was the thirteenth race of the 2000 Formula One season, and the 58th Belgian Grand Prix.
The 44-lap race was won by McLaren driver Mika Häkkinen, who started from the pole position.
Michael Schumacher finished second in a Ferrari, and Williams driver Ralf Schumacher was third.
The 2014 Japanese Grand Prix (formally the 2014 Formula 1 Japanese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 5 October at the Suzuka Circuit in Suzuka, Mie.
It was the fifteenth round of the 2014 Formula One season and the 30th Japanese Grand Prix held as part of the Formula One World Championship.
The 44-lap race was won by Mercedes driver Lewis Hamilton, who started from second position.
His teammate, Nico Rosberg, finished second and Red Bull Racing driver Sebastian Vettel came in third.
It was Hamilton's eighth victory of the season, his first at Suzuka and the 30th of his Formula One career.
The 2016 Belgian Grand Prix (formally known as the 2016 Formula 1 Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 28 August 2016 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the thirteenth round of the 2016 Formula One season, and marks the seventy-second running of the Belgian Grand Prix and the fifty-ninth time the race was held at Spa-Francorchamps.
The 2015 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the 2015 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 23 August 2015 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the eleventh round of the 2015 Formula One season, and the 71st Belgian Grand Prix.
The 2014 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the 2014 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 24 August 2014 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the twelfth round of the 2014 Formula One season, and the 70th Belgian Grand Prix.
The 44-lap race was won by Daniel Ricciardo for the Red Bull Racing team, after starting from fifth position.
Nico Rosberg finished second in a Mercedes, with Valtteri Bottas third for the Williams team.
The 2008 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXIV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 7 September 2008 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.
It was the 13th race of the 2008 Formula One season.

The 44-lap race was won by Felipe Massa for the Ferrari team, after the initial winner, McLaren driver Lewis Hamilton, was penalised for cutting a chicane and gaining an advantage over Ferrari's Kimi Räikkönen.
The 2005 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXII Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 11 September 2005 at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the sixteenth round of the 2005 Formula One season and the sixty-second Belgian Grand Prix.
The 44-lap race was won by Kimi Räikkönen for the McLaren team, from a second position start.
Fernando Alonso finished second in a Renault, with Jenson Button third for the BAR team.
The 2011 Belgian Grand Prix, formally the 2011 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix, was a Formula One motor race that was held on 28 August 2011, at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.
It was the twelfth round of the 2011 Formula One season and the 67th Belgian Grand Prix to be held.
The 44-lap race was won by Red Bull's Sebastian Vettel, the drivers' championship leader, after starting from pole position.
Vettel's teammate Mark Webber finished in second place, and Jenson Button completed the podium in third position for McLaren.
The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.
It was the 12th race of the 2009 Formula One season.
The race was contested over 44 laps, with an overall race distance of 308.2 km (191.5 mi).
The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen of Ferrari after starting from sixth on the grid.
Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second after starting from the team's first pole position.
Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.
Sebastian Vettel (] ; born 3 July 1987) is a German racing driver currently driving in Formula One for Scuderia Ferrari.

He is a four-time Formula One World Champion, having won the championship in , , and with Red Bull Racing, therefore being among the most successful F1 drivers of all time, as he is one of only four drivers to have won four or more drivers' titles.
He is regarded by fellow and former drivers as one of the greatest Formula One drivers in the history of the sport.
Vettel signed a three-year contract with Ferrari upon his arrival, a contract later extended until the end of 2020.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on which date, Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid?
hop1_docs: 2009 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.  It was the 12th race of the 2009 Formula One season.  The race was contested over 44 laps, with an overall race distance of 308.2 km (191.5 mi).  The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen of Ferrari after starting from sixth on the grid.  Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second after starting from the team's first pole position.  Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.

2011 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2011 Belgian Grand Prix, formally the 2011 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix, was a Formula One motor race that was held on 28 August 2011, at Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.  It was the twelfth round of the 2011 Formula One season and the 67th Belgian Grand Prix to be held.  The 44-lap race was won by Red Bull's Sebastian Vettel, the drivers' championship leader, after starting from pole position.  Vettel's teammate Mark Webber finished in second place, and Jenson Button completed the podium in third position for McLaren.

2011 Japanese Grand Prix | The 2011 Japanese Grand Prix (formally the XXXVII Japanese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race that was held on 9 October 2011 at the Suzuka Circuit in Suzuka, Japan.  It was the fifteenth round of the 2011 Formula One season and the thirty-eighth time the Japanese Grand Prix had been held.  The 53-lap race was won by McLaren's Jenson Button, after he started from second on the grid.  Fernando Alonso finished in second place for Scuderia Ferrari, and Sebastian Vettel completed the podium, with third, for Red Bull Racing.

2011 Hungarian Grand Prix | The 2011 Hungarian Grand Prix, formally the XXVII Eni Magyar Nagydíj, was a Formula One motor race that was held on 31 July 2011 at the Hungaroring in Mogyoród, near Budapest, Hungary It was the eleventh round of the 2011 Formula One season, and the 27th Hungarian Grand Prix.  The 70-lap race was won by McLaren's Jenson Button, in his 200th Grand Prix start, after starting from third on the grid.  Championship leader Sebastian Vettel, who started the race from pole position, finished in second place for Red Bull, and Fernando Alonso completed the podium in third position for Ferrari.

2011 Chinese Grand Prix | The 2011 Chinese Grand Prix (formally the 2011 Formula 1 UBS Chinese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 17 April 2011 at the Shanghai International Circuit in Shanghai, People's Republic of China.  It was the third round of the 2011 Formula One season.  The 56-lap race was won by McLaren driver Lewis Hamilton after starting from third on the grid, also becoming the first ever multiple winner of the Chinese Grand Prix.  Sebastian Vettel finished second in a Red Bull, having started from pole position, and teammate Mark Webber completed the podium, in third place having started eighteenth.

2011 European Grand Prix | The 2011 European Grand Prix (formally the LV Grand Prix of Europe) was a Formula One motor race held on 26 June 2011 at the Valencia Street Circuit in Valencia, Spain.  The race, which was the eighth round of the 2011 Formula One season, was won by defending world drivers' champion and championship leader Sebastian Vettel, who was driving a Red Bull car.  Vettel, who started from pole position, also recorded the fastest lap of the race on lap 53, giving him a hat-trick.  After a race-long battle, Ferrari driver Fernando Alonso and Vettel's teammate Mark Webber finished the race in second and third respectively.  The race was notable for having the fewest retirements and the most finishers ever in a Formula One Grand Prix.

2014 Japanese Grand Prix | The 2014 Japanese Grand Prix (formally the 2014 Formula 1 Japanese Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 5 October at the Suzuka Circuit in Suzuka, Mie.  It was the fifteenth round of the 2014 Formula One season and the 30th Japanese Grand Prix held as part of the Formula One World Championship.  The 44-lap race was won by Mercedes driver Lewis Hamilton, who started from second position.  His teammate, Nico Rosberg, finished second and Red Bull Racing driver Sebastian Vettel came in third.  It was Hamilton's eighth victory of the season, his first at Suzuka and the 30th of his Formula One career.

- "Kimi Räikkönen" — [1] — "The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen"
- "Giancarlo Fisichella" — [1] — "Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second"

Ambiguities:
- "Belgian Grand Prix" corresponds to multiple yearly pages in the passages: "2009 Belgian Grand Prix" [1] and "2011 Belgian Grand Prix" [2].
- "Sebastian Vettel" appears across many race pages ([1],[2],[3],[4],[5],[6],[7]) but passage [1] unambiguously gives his 2009 result.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts for the question: the race date for the 2009 Belgian Grand Prix is explicitly given as "30 August 2009" in passage [1].

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "2009 Belgian Grand Prix" (the passage already cited).
- (Optional verification) Open page "Sebastian Vettel".
- (Optional) Open page "Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps".
Final conclusion: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix was held on 30 August 2009.
hop2_docs: 1925 Belgian Grand Prix | The 1925 Belgian Grand Prix was a Grand Prix motor race held at Spa-Francorchamps on 28 June 1925.  This was the first ever grand prix held at Spa, and the first ever Belgian Grand Prix.

2009 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the LXV ING Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 30 August 2009 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps near the town of Spa, Belgium.  It was the 12th race of the 2009 Formula One season.  The race was contested over 44 laps, with an overall race distance of 308.2 km (191.5 mi).  The race was won by 2007 world champion Kimi Räikkönen of Ferrari after starting from sixth on the grid.  Giancarlo Fisichella of Force India came second after starting from the team's first pole position.  Sebastian Vettel came third after starting from eighth on the grid.

1984 Belgian Grand Prix | The 1984 Belgian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held on 29 April 1984.  It was the third round of the 1984 Formula One season.  The race was the 42nd Belgian Grand Prix and the tenth and last Grand Prix to be held at Circuit Zolder.  The race was held over 70 laps of the four kilometre circuit for a race distance of 298 kilometres.

2016 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2016 Belgian Grand Prix (formally known as the 2016 Formula 1 Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 28 August 2016 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.  It was the thirteenth round of the 2016 Formula One season, and marks the seventy-second running of the Belgian Grand Prix and the fifty-ninth time the race was held at Spa-Francorchamps.
1937 Belgian Grand Prix | The 1937 Belgian Grand Prix was a motor race held at Spa-Francorchamps on 11 July 1937.  It was the last Grand Prix to be held at the circuit's original configuration.

2015 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2015 Belgian Grand Prix (formally the 2015 Formula 1 Shell Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race held on 23 August 2015 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Spa, Belgium.  It was the eleventh round of the 2015 Formula One season, and the 71st Belgian Grand Prix.

2017 Belgian Grand Prix | The 2017 Belgian Grand Prix (formally known as the 2017 Formula 1 Pirelli Belgian Grand Prix) was a Formula One motor race that was held on 27 August 2017 at the Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Stavelot, Belgium.  The race, scheduled to be contested over forty-four laps, was the twelfth round of the 2017 Formula One season, and marked the seventy-third running of the Belgian Grand Prix and the sixtieth time the race was held at Spa-Francorchamps.
response.summary_2: The 2009 Belgian Grand Prix was held on 30 August 2009.
